questionText: If the coefficients of the middle terms in the binomial expansions of $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$ and $(1-\alpha x)^{28}, \alpha \neq 0$, are equal, then the value of $\alpha$ is:
subject: Mathematics
solution:
For a binomial expansion $(a+b)^n$:

  • if $n$ is even, there is only one middle term,

  • and the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{n}{2}+1\right)$th term.

We are given:

$$ (1+\alpha x)^{26} \quad \text{and} \quad (1-\alpha x)^{28} $$

and the coefficients of their middle terms are equal.

1. Middle term of $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$

Since $26$ is even, the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{26}{2}+1\right)=14$th term.

The general term in $(1+\alpha x)^{26}$ is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{26}{r}(1)^{26-r}(\alpha x)^r $$

So the $14$th term corresponds to $r=13$:

$$ T_{14}=\binom{26}{13}(\alpha x)^{13} $$

Hence, the coefficient of the middle term is

$$ \binom{26}{13}\alpha^{13} $$

2. Middle term of $(1-\alpha x)^{28}$

Since $28$ is even, the middle term is the $\left(\dfrac{28}{2}+1\right)=15$th term.

The general term is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{28}{r}(1)^{28-r}(-\alpha x)^r $$

So the $15$th term corresponds to $r=14$:

$$ T_{15}=\binom{28}{14}(-\alpha x)^{14} $$

Since $14$ is even,

$$ (-\alpha x)^{14}=\alpha^{14}x^{14} $$

Thus, the coefficient of the middle term is

$$ \binom{28}{14}\alpha^{14} $$

3. Equate the coefficients

Given that these coefficients are equal:

$$ \binom{26}{13}\alpha^{13}=\binom{28}{14}\alpha^{14} $$

Since $\alpha\neq 0$, divide both sides by $\alpha^{13}$:

$$ \binom{26}{13}=\binom{28}{14}\alpha $$

So,

$$ \alpha=\frac{\binom{26}{13}}{\binom{28}{14}} $$

4. Simplify

$$ \alpha=\frac{\dfrac{26!}{13!13!}}{\dfrac{28!}{14!14!}} $$

$$ \alpha=\frac{26!}{13!13!}\cdot \frac{14!14!}{28!} $$

Now use

$$ 14!=14\cdot 13! $$

So,

$$ 14!14!=(14\cdot 13!)(14\cdot 13!)=14^2(13!)^2 $$

Thus,

$$ \alpha=\frac{26!\cdot 14^2(13!)^2}{(13!)^2\cdot 28!} =\frac{26!\cdot 14^2}{28!} $$

Also,

$$ 28!=28\cdot 27\cdot 26! $$

Therefore,

$$ \alpha=\frac{14^2}{28\cdot 27} =\frac{14}{2\cdot 27} =\frac{7}{27} $$

$$ \boxed{\alpha=\frac{7}{27}} $$

So, the correct option is D.

questionText: Let K be the sum of the coefficients of the odd powers of $$x$$ in the expansion of $$(1+x)^{99}$$. Let $$a$$ be the middle term in the expansion of $${\left( {2 + {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)^{200}}$$. If $${{{}^{200}{C_{99}}K} \over a} = {{{2^l}m} \over n}$$, where m and n are odd numbers, then the ordered pair $$(l,\mathrm{n})$$ is equal to
subject: Mathematics

questionText: Let the coefficients of the middle terms in the expansion of $$\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}+\beta x\right)^{4},(1-3 \beta x)^{2}$$ and $$\left(1-\frac{\beta}{2} x\right)^{6}, \beta>0$$, respectively form the first three terms of an A.P. If d is the common difference of this A.P. , then $$50-\frac{2 d}{\beta^{2}}$$ is equal to __________.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Coefficients of middle terms of given expansions are $${}^4{C_2}{1 \over 6}{\beta ^2},\,{}^2{C_1}( - 3\beta ),\,{}^6{C_3}{\left( {{{ - \beta } \over 2}} \right)^3}$$ form an A.P.

$$\therefore$$ $$2.2( - 3\beta ) = {\beta ^2} - {{5{\beta ^3}} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 24 = 2\beta - 5{\beta ^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 5{\beta ^2} - 2\beta - 24 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 5{\beta ^2} - 12\beta + 10\beta - 24 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \beta (5\beta - 12) + 2(5\beta - 12) = 0$$

$$\beta = {{12} \over 5}$$

$$d = - 6\beta - {\beta ^2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$50 - {{2d} \over {{\beta ^2}}} = 50 - 2{{( - 6\beta - {\beta ^2})} \over {{\beta ^2}}} = 50 + {{12} \over \beta } + 2 = 57$$

questionText: The ratio of the coefficient of the middle term in the expansion of (1 + x)<sup>20</sup> and the sum of the coefficients of two middle terms in expansion of (1 + x)<sup>19</sup> is _____________.

questionText: The coefficient of the middle term in the binomial expansion in powers of $$x$$ of $${\left( {1 + \alpha x} \right)^4}$$ and $${\left( {1 - \alpha x} \right)^6}$$  is the same if $$\alpha $$ equals
subject: Mathematics
solution:
For $${\left( {1 + \alpha x} \right)^4}$$ the middle term $${T_{{4 \over 2} + 1}}$$ = $${}^4{C_2}.{\alpha ^2}{x^2}$$

$$\therefore$$ Coefficient of middle term = $${}^4{C_2}.{\alpha ^2}$$

For $${\left( {1 - \alpha x} \right)^6}$$ the middle term $${T_{{6 \over 2} + 1}}$$ = $${}^6{C_3}.-{\alpha ^3}{x^3}$$

$$\therefore$$ Coefficient of middle term = $${}^6{C_3}.{-\alpha ^3}$$

$$\therefore$$ According to question,

$${}^4{C_2}.{\alpha ^2}$$ = $${}^6{C_3}.{-\alpha ^3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 6 = 20 \times - \alpha $$

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha = - {3 \over {10}}$$
